1.title: Skeletal muscle architecture & fiber types
1.key_concept: Skeletal muscle is organized hierarchically: muscle → fasciculus → muscle fiber (one cell) → myofibril → sarcomere. The sarcomere (Z-disc to Z-disc) is the contractile unit; sliding-filament theory says actin slides along myosin via cross-bridge cycling, powered by ATP. Fibers are classified by myosin heavy chain isoform: Type I (slow oxidative, fatigue-resistant, low force, red), Type IIa (fast oxidative-glycolytic, intermediate), Type IIx (fast glycolytic, highest force, fatigues fast). Training shifts IIx → IIa; fiber-type proportion is largely genetic but trainable in expression.
key_facts: Sarcomere = Z-disc to Z-disc
Cross-bridge cycle requires ATP for both attachment AND detachment
Type I: high mitochondrial density, capillaries, myoglobin
Type IIx: largest cross-sectional area, highest force/velocity, lowest endurance
Heavy resistance training shifts IIx → IIa (not Type II → Type I)
1.training_link: Your aerobic base (HR 132 indoor Z2 cap, LT1=147) reflects Type I oxidative capacity. Your 4×8 sessions (HR 170-175 target) recruit Type IIa heavily — sub-MLSS endurance work develops the IIa pool you need for Hyrox station blocks.

glossary.def: The contractile unit, Z-disc to Z-disc. When muscle contracts, sarcomeres shorten — the thin filaments slide inward past the thick filament.

glossary.def: Thick filament (blue). Its 'heads' grab actin and pull — the cross-bridge cycle.
Thin filament (green). Anchored at Z-discs, projects toward myosin. Has binding sites for myosin heads.

<svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="16" fill="#a04040" opacity="0.8"/><circle cx="32" cy="14" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="44" cy="12" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="48" cy="24" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="30" cy="26" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="3" fill="#ffb86b"/></svg>

glossary.def: Pale, glycogen-rich, highest force + speed but fatigues fast. Sprint fibers.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="40" cy="20" r="16" fill="#f0d8a0" opacity="0.85"/><g stroke="#ffd84a" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="round" fill="none"><path d="M 30 14 L 50 14 M 28 20 L 52 20 M 30 26 L 50 26"/></g></svg>

2.title: Motor units & the size principle
2.key_concept: A motor unit = one alpha motoneuron + all the fibers it innervates. All fibers in a single motor unit are the same type. The Size Principle (Henneman, 1965): motor units are recruited in order of size — smallest (Type I) first, largest (Type IIx) last, as force demand rises. Rate coding (firing frequency) and recruitment together grade force. Heavier loads or maximal-effort actions recruit higher-threshold units; this is why high-intensity work is non-negotiable for Type II development.
key_facts: Henneman's Size Principle — small to large recruitment order
Force gradation = recruitment + rate coding
All fibers in a motor unit are SAME type
High-threshold MUs recruited only at ~80%+ of max force OR at maximal velocities even with lighter loads
Doublet firing (2 rapid impulses) enhances rate of force development
2.training_link: Your strength PM sessions need true high-intensity exposure to recruit Type IIx motor units. Z2 cycling never gets there — that's why bike day's strength block is structurally non-substitutable.

glossary.def: One alpha motoneuron plus every fiber it innervates. All fibers in a unit are the same type.

glossary.def: Smallest (Type I) motor units fire first. Larger (Type IIa, then IIx) units recruit only as force demand rises.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="35" r="6" fill="#67e8b0"/><circle cx="40" cy="32" r="9" fill="#ffb86b"/><circle cx="65" cy="28" r="13" fill="#ff7a7a"/><line x1="10" y1="45" x2="90" y2="45" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5"/><text x="50" y="49" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="6" text-anchor="middle">Force demand →</text></svg>

glossary.def: After all units are recruited, force keeps rising via higher firing frequency of motoneurons.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 40" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="1.5" fill="none"><path d="M 10 25 L 18 25 L 18 12 L 22 12 L 22 25 L 30 25"/><path d="M 35 25 L 40 25 L 40 12 L 43 12 L 43 25 L 48 25 L 48 12 L 51 12 L 51 25 L 56 25"/><path d="M 60 25 L 64 25 L 64 12 L 66 12 L 66 25 L 70 25 L 70 12 L 72 12 L 72 25 L 76 25 L 76 12 L 78 12 L 78 25 L 85 25"/></g><text x="20" y="38" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">low</text><text x="48" y="38" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">moderate</text><text x="73" y="38" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">high</text></svg>

3.title: Neuromuscular junction & excitation-contraction coupling
3.key_concept: Action potential travels down motoneuron → acetylcholine (ACh) released at neuromuscular junction → ACh binds nicotinic receptors on sarcolemma → AP propagates down T-tubules → triggers sarcoplasmic reticulum to release Ca²⁺ → Ca²⁺ binds troponin → tropomyosin shifts → myosin binds actin → cross-bridge cycle. Relaxation requires ATP-dependent Ca²⁺ reuptake into SR. This is why ATP depletion causes rigor (no detachment).
key_facts: ACh is the neurotransmitter at the NMJ
Ca²⁺ release from SR is the immediate trigger for contraction
Troponin C binds Ca²⁺; tropomyosin physically blocks myosin-binding sites on actin
ATP is required for cross-bridge DETACHMENT (rigor mortis = no ATP)
Ca²⁺ reuptake is also ATP-dependent (SERCA pump)
3.training_link: Caffeine enhances Ca²⁺ release from SR — one mechanism behind its ergogenic effect on your cycling and 4×8 sessions.

glossary.def: The neurotransmitter at the neuromuscular junction. Binds nicotinic receptors on the sarcolemma, triggering depolarization.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="25" r="10" fill="#5ec8ff" opacity="0.3"/><circle cx="20" cy="25" r="4" fill="#5ec8ff"/><g fill="#ffd84a"><circle cx="35" cy="20" r="2.5"/><circle cx="45" cy="28" r="2.5"/><circle cx="55" cy="22" r="2.5"/></g><rect x="65" y="15" width="25" height="20" fill="#a04040" opacity="0.4" rx="3"/><g fill="#a04040"><circle cx="73" cy="22" r="2"/><circle cx="82" cy="22" r="2"/><circle cx="73" cy="30" r="2"/><circle cx="82" cy="30" r="2"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: Invagination of sarcolemma that carries action potentials into the depth of the muscle fiber.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="5" y="5" width="90" height="40" fill="none" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2" rx="2"/><line x1="30" y1="5" x2="30" y2="40" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="55" y1="5" x2="55" y2="40" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="80" y1="5" x2="80" y2="40" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

glossary.def: Intracellular calcium store. Action potentials release Ca²⁺ from the SR; the Ca²⁺ triggers contraction.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M 10 25 Q 25 10 50 25 T 90 25" fill="none" stroke="#67e8b0" stroke-width="2.5"/><g fill="#ffb86b"><circle cx="20" cy="20" r="2"/><circle cx="35" cy="14" r="2"/><circle cx="50" cy="20" r="2"/><circle cx="65" cy="14" r="2"/><circle cx="80" cy="20" r="2"/></g><text x="50" y="45" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="6" text-anchor="middle">Ca²⁺ released</text></svg>

glossary.def: Myosin head binds actin → power stroke → ATP detaches it → ATP hydrolysis re-cocks it. Requires ATP for BOTH attachment and detachment.

4.title: Cardiovascular system — heart, vessels, blood flow
4.key_concept: Cardiac output (Q) = heart rate × stroke volume. At rest ~5 L/min; max exercise ~20-40 L/min. Stroke volume rises until ~40-60% VO₂max then plateaus; HR rises linearly with intensity. Endurance training increases SV (eccentric LV hypertrophy = larger chamber) lowering resting HR. Resistance training causes concentric hypertrophy (thicker wall). Arteries → arterioles (resistance) → capillaries (exchange) → venules → veins. Baroreceptors and chemoreceptors regulate BP/HR.
key_facts: Q = HR × SV
Frank-Starling: increased venous return → increased SV (stretched LV)
Endurance training: ↑SV via ↑LV chamber size (eccentric hypertrophy)
Resistance training: ↑LV wall thickness (concentric hypertrophy)
Capillary density increases with aerobic training
4.training_link: Your RHR drop 46→37 bpm and HRV rise 118→187 ms (Jan→May) is classic endurance adaptation — larger SV requires lower HR for the same Q. Your LT1=147 with HR cap 132 indoor for Z2 reflects this trained state.

video.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training 101 (cardiac adaptations)

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #85 — Iñigo San-Millán: Mitochondria, Exercise, and Metabolic Health

5.title: Respiratory system & gas exchange
5.key_concept: Pulmonary ventilation = breathing frequency × tidal volume. Gas exchange occurs at alveoli via diffusion (driven by partial pressure gradients). O₂ binds hemoglobin (~98.5% saturation at sea level). The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve shifts RIGHT with ↑temp, ↑CO₂, ↓pH (Bohr effect) — promoting O₂ unloading at working muscle. CO₂ is transported as bicarbonate (~70%), bound to Hb (~20%), and dissolved (~10%).
key_facts: Bohr effect: right shift in O₂-Hb curve unloads O₂ at active muscle
Resting ventilation: ~6 L/min; max exercise: ~150+ L/min
Tidal volume rises first, then frequency, with increasing intensity
VO₂max is typically NOT pulmonary-limited in healthy non-elites
Ventilatory threshold (VT) tracks lactate threshold
5.training_link: Your ventilatory pattern shifts at LT1=147 (indoor) — that's the VT1 inflection. Above LT2=158, breathing becomes the rate-limiter, not legs.

video.title: Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve (animation)

6.title: Bioenergetics I — ATP and the phosphagen system
6.key_concept: ATP is the energy currency. Stored ATP lasts ~2 seconds of max work. The phosphagen system (ATP-PCr) regenerates ATP via creatine kinase: PCr + ADP → ATP + Cr. Dominant for ~0-10s all-out efforts (jumps, throws, 1RM, sprint start). PCr stores fully replenish in ~3-5 min rest. This is why creatine supplementation works — it expands the PCr pool.
key_facts: ATP-PCr system: anaerobic, no lactate, fastest power production
Duration: ~0-10 seconds at max effort
Creatine kinase enzyme
Recovery: ~30s for ~50%, full ~3-5 min
Creatine supplementation: 3-5 g/day; ~20% PCr increase
6.training_link: Your strength PM work (heavy compound lifts) is phosphagen-dominant. Why 3-5 min inter-set rest matters: that's the PCr replenishment window. Cutting rest to 60s drops Type II force output.

glossary.def: Adenosine triphosphate. The cell's energy currency. Hydrolysis to ADP + Pi releases the energy that powers contraction.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="25" r="9" fill="#ffd84a"/><text x="20" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="8" font-weight="700">A</text><circle cx="40" cy="25" r="7" fill="#ffb86b"/><text x="40" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">P</text><circle cx="55" cy="25" r="7" fill="#ffb86b"/><text x="55" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">P</text><circle cx="70" cy="25" r="7" fill="#ffb86b"/><text x="70" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">P</text><g stroke="#a37a08" stroke-width="1"><line x1="29" y1="25" x2="33" y2="25"/><line x1="47" y1="25" x2="48" y2="25"/><line x1="62" y1="25" x2="63" y2="25"/></g></svg>

glossary.def: High-energy phosphate stored in muscle. Creatine kinase transfers its phosphate to ADP → ATP. Fastest way to regenerate ATP.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="35" cy="25" r="12" fill="#ff8a3a" opacity="0.85"/><text x="35" y="22" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a1a00" font-size="7" font-weight="700">Cr</text><text x="35" y="32" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a1a00" font-size="7" font-weight="700">~P</text><path d="M 50 25 L 65 25" stroke="#ffd84a" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><defs><marker id="arr" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="5" refY="5" markerWidth="4" markerHeight="4" orient="auto"><polygon points="0,0 10,5 0,10" fill="#ffd84a"/></marker></defs><circle cx="78" cy="25" r="10" fill="#ffd84a"/><text x="78" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">ATP</text></svg>

glossary.def: Enzyme that catalyzes PCr + ADP → ATP + Cr. Very fast reaction — the phosphagen system supplies energy in milliseconds.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><g><circle cx="50" cy="25" r="18" fill="#ffd84a" opacity="0.3"/><circle cx="50" cy="25" r="14" fill="#ffd84a" opacity="0.5"/><circle cx="50" cy="25" r="10" fill="#ffd84a"/><text x="50" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#3a2008" font-size="7" font-weight="700">CK</text></g></svg>

7.title: Bioenergetics II — Glycolysis & lactate
7.key_concept: Glycolysis splits glucose (or glycogen) → pyruvate, yielding 2 ATP (from glucose; 3 from glycogen). Fast glycolysis: pyruvate → lactate when oxygen-limited or rate of demand exceeds mitochondrial capacity. Slow glycolysis: pyruvate → acetyl-CoA → Krebs/ETC. Lactate is NOT waste — it's a fuel (Brooks' lactate shuttle), shuttled to oxidative fibers, heart, and liver (Cori cycle: lactate → glucose). MLSS (maximal lactate steady state) is the ceiling of sustainable steady-state work; tracks LT2.
key_facts: Net ATP yield: 2 (glucose) or 3 (glycogen)
Lactate is a fuel and signaling molecule, NOT just waste
MLSS ≈ LT2 ≈ critical power (~1 hour TT pace)
Lactate threshold 1 (LT1, ~2 mmol/L) = aerobic threshold; LT2 (~4 mmol/L) = anaerobic threshold (rough heuristics; individual)
Cori cycle: muscle lactate → liver glucose → back to muscle
7.training_link: Your individual LT1=147, LT2=158 indoor anchor your zones. 4×8 work at HR 170-175 sits supra-MLSS — that's WHY it stresses the system: above lactate steady state, accumulation forces metabolic adaptation.

glossary.def: Breakdown of glucose → 2 pyruvate, yielding 2 ATP (3 from glycogen). 'Fast' glycolysis sends pyruvate to lactate when oxygen is limiting.

glossary.def: Pyruvate + NADH → lactate. NOT waste — Brooks lactate shuttle: lactate moves to oxidative fibers, heart, liver for fuel.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="20" cy="25" r="8" fill="#ff8a3a"/><line x1="28" y1="25" x2="48" y2="25" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="56" cy="25" r="10" fill="#a04040"/><text x="56" y="28" text-anchor="middle" fill="#fff" font-size="6">muscle</text><line x1="66" y1="25" x2="80" y2="25" stroke="#5ec8ff" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><circle cx="88" cy="25" r="6" fill="#ff5e5e"/><text x="50" y="45" text-anchor="middle" fill="#9ba3b4" font-size="5">shuttle to heart/liver</text></svg>

glossary.def: Highest intensity where lactate production = lactate clearance. Tracks LT2. Your LT2 = 158 bpm indoor.
glossary.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 100 50" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="10" y1="40" x2="90" y2="40" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="10" y1="40" x2="10" y2="5" stroke="#9ba3b4" stroke-width="0.5"/><path d="M 10 38 L 25 36 L 40 32 L 50 28 L 58 18 L 70 8 L 85 5" fill="none" stroke="#ffb86b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="55" y1="5" x2="55" y2="40" stroke="#ff5e5e" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="2 2"/><text x="55" y="50" text-anchor="middle" fill="#ff5e5e" font-size="5">LT2 / MLSS</text></svg>

8.title: Bioenergetics III — Oxidative phosphorylation
8.key_concept: Aerobic ATP production in mitochondria: Krebs cycle generates NADH/FADH₂; electron transport chain uses O₂ as the final electron acceptor to produce ~30-32 ATP per glucose (vs 2 from glycolysis alone). Fat oxidation yields more ATP per molecule but requires more O₂. Fuel selection shifts with intensity: low intensity = predominantly fat; rising intensity = more carb. Crossover point ~65% VO₂max where carb > fat contribution.
key_facts: Aerobic ATP yield: ~30-32 per glucose, ~100+ per fatty acid
Final electron acceptor: O₂ → forms water
Crossover point: ~65% VO₂max, carb contribution surpasses fat
RER = VCO₂/VO₂: 0.7 (pure fat) → 1.0 (pure carb)
Mitochondrial density doubles with aerobic training
8.training_link: Z2 (HR 132 indoor cap) maximally stimulates fat oxidation enzymes and mitochondrial biogenesis without lactate accumulation. The 'long, slow' isn't slow — it's metabolically targeted.

video.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training 201: Past, Present & Future

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — San-Millán on Zone 2 & Mitochondrial Function

9.title: Energy system interaction & substrate use
9.key_concept: All three systems are ALWAYS active — proportions shift with duration and intensity. EPOC (excess post-exercise O₂ consumption) repays the 'O₂ debt' — replenishing PCr, restoring O₂ to myoglobin/blood, clearing lactate, and thermoregulation. Training-induced shifts: glycogen storage capacity increases, mitochondrial density doubles, capillarization improves, lactate clearance improves at given intensity.
key_facts: 0-10s: ATP-PCr dominant
10s-2min: fast glycolysis dominant
2min-3hr: oxidative dominant (carb early, fat later)
>3hr: fat oxidation increasingly dominant
EPOC magnitude scales with intensity, not just duration
9.training_link: Your Hyrox sessions tax all three systems concurrently — sled push (ATP-PCr/glycolytic), run intervals (oxidative-glycolytic), station work (mixed). That's why recovery cost is 3 days.

video.title: Most of Your Training Should Be EASY! Polarized Training With Dr. Stephen Seiler

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #85 — San-Millán: Energy Systems

10.title: Biomechanics I — Force, torque, levers
10.key_concept: Torque = force × moment arm (perpendicular distance from line of force to axis of rotation). Body joints function as levers. Most human joints are 3rd-class levers (effort between fulcrum and load — biceps curl, leg extension): mechanical disadvantage for force, advantage for range/speed. 1st-class (fulcrum between, e.g., triceps), 2nd-class (load between, e.g., calf raise) also exist. Resistance training requires understanding where torque demand peaks across the ROM (sticking points).
key_facts: Torque = F × d (moment arm)
3rd-class lever: most joints, e.g., elbow flexion (biceps)
Moment arm changes through ROM — peak torque demand often mid-range
Long limbs = larger moment arms = greater torque required
Pulley systems and cams (Nautilus machines) attempt to match torque curves
10.training_link: Sticking point in squat (just out of hole) reflects worst leverage where torque demand peaks. Programming pause squats targets that exact ROM.

video.title: How To Squat Correctly (FIX MISTAKES!) — Squat University

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Optimal Protocols to Build Strength & Grow Muscles

11.title: Biomechanics II — Force-velocity & length-tension
11.key_concept: Force-velocity curve: muscle produces MORE force at slow speeds (concentric) and even more eccentrically. As velocity rises, concentric force drops. Power = force × velocity; peak power at ~30% of max isometric force. Length-tension: actin-myosin overlap is optimal at ~resting length; force drops at extremes (too short = overlap interference; too long = insufficient overlap).
key_facts: Eccentric > isometric > concentric force production
Peak power at ~30% max isometric force
Length-tension optimum near resting length
Stretch-shortening cycle: eccentric pre-load enhances concentric output (plyometrics)
Velocity-based training uses this curve to prescribe load
11.training_link: Your strength PM heavy work (high force, low velocity) develops the left side of F-V curve; plyos/jumps would develop the right side. For Hyrox-specific power-endurance, you need both.

video.title: Dr. Andy Galpin: Muscle Fiber Types and Muscle Growth (force-velocity context)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: How to Build Strength, Muscle Size & Endurance

12.title: Endocrine I — Anabolic hormones
12.key_concept: Key anabolic hormones: testosterone (gonads/adrenals, ↑ protein synthesis, ↑ neural drive), GH (anterior pituitary, ↑ IGF-1 release, ↑ collagen/fat utilization), IGF-1 (liver + muscle, mediates much of GH's anabolic action), insulin (pancreas, ↑ nutrient uptake, anti-catabolic). Acute resistance exercise spikes T, GH, and IGF-1 — magnitude scales with: total volume, large muscle masses worked, short rest, moderate-high intensity. Chronic resting levels matter less than acute training response for hypertrophy.
key_facts: Testosterone — primary androgen; produced gonads (Leydig cells) and adrenals
GH released in pulses, peaks in deep sleep stages
IGF-1 mediates many of GH's actions
Acute spike correlates with hypertrophy; chronic levels less so
High volume + short rest + large muscles = biggest acute response
12.training_link: Sleep is THE limiter for you (median 5.5h vs 9.8h need). GH peaks in slow-wave sleep — short sleep blunts the anabolic environment your training creates.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Updated View on Mechanisms of Muscle Hypertrophy (Iron Culture 181)

audio.title: Stronger By Science Podcast — Episode 9: Hormones & Training Adaptations
audio.credibility: Greg Nuckols MSc + Eric Trexler PhD

13.title: Endocrine II — Catabolic hormones & stress
13.key_concept: Cortisol (adrenal cortex): primary glucocorticoid. Released in response to stress, including exercise. Elevates blood glucose (gluconeogenesis), mobilizes fat, BUT chronically elevated impairs protein synthesis and immune function. T:C ratio used as overtraining marker. Catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine) from adrenal medulla — fight-or-flight, ↑HR, ↑BP, glycogenolysis.
key_facts: Cortisol = glucocorticoid; circadian peak ~AM
Acute exercise cortisol rise is normal and adaptive
Chronic elevation = problem (overtraining, poor sleep, undereating)
T:C ratio drop ≥30% suggests overtraining
Epinephrine peaks during high-intensity work
13.training_link: Your HRV trend (118→187 ms) is a downstream marker of autonomic balance — high HRV = parasympathetic dominance = lower catabolic load. Sleep debt and heat (33°C cost more recovery than 4×8s) push the other direction.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Mechanisms of Muscle Hypertrophy (Iron Culture 181)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Recovery & Stress

14.title: Anaerobic training adaptations
14.key_concept: Neural adaptations dominate weeks 1-4: improved motor unit recruitment, rate coding, synchronization, decreased Golgi tendon organ inhibition. Hypertrophy starts ~4-6 weeks, becomes dominant after 8+. Muscle: ↑CSA, mostly Type II preferential, ↑ contractile protein, ↑ phosphagen stores, ↑ glycolytic enzymes. Connective tissue: ↑ tendon stiffness, ↑ collagen synthesis. Bone: ↑ mineral density. Endocrine: enhanced acute hormonal response. Limited cardiovascular changes (mostly concentric LV hypertrophy).
key_facts: First 4 wks: neural > hypertrophic
Hypertrophy: ~4-6+ wks to become evident
Type II fibers hypertrophy preferentially with heavy load
Connective tissue lags muscle — injury risk in rapid load jumps
Cross-sectional area is primary determinant of force
14.training_link: Your strength PM has been consistent for months — past neural-only phase, into structural. Connective-tissue lag is why station work substitutions for Hyrox B must respect joint history.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Maximizing Muscle Hypertrophy (Iron Culture 51)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Strength & Muscle Building Protocols

15.title: Aerobic endurance training adaptations
15.key_concept: Central (cardiac): ↑LV chamber size (eccentric hypertrophy), ↑SV, ↓RHR. Peripheral: ↑mitochondrial density and size, ↑oxidative enzymes (citrate synthase, SDH), ↑capillary density, ↑myoglobin, ↑fat oxidation capacity, shifted fuel use toward fat at submax intensities. VO₂max plateaus in ~6-12 months of well-designed training; LT improves longer. Detraining: cardiac changes regress in ~2-4 weeks; mitochondrial in similar timeframe.
key_facts: VO₂max = Q × (a-vO₂ difference)
VO₂max improves 5-20% in untrained; less in already-trained
Lactate threshold trainable over years even after VO₂max plateaus
Capillary density and mitochondrial density both ~2× with chronic training
Detraining: ~50% loss of central adaptations in 2-4 wks of inactivity
15.training_link: Your Jan→May trajectory (HRV +59%, RHR -9) IS this curve in action. With base solidified, LT improvements are the next frontier — sub-threshold 10×1000m and 4×8 (above MLSS) are the right tools.

video.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training 101

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — Zone 2 Deep Dive (Part 2)

16.title: Age and sex differences in training response
16.key_concept: Children: can train safely with proper supervision; resistance training does NOT stunt growth. Pre-pubertal gains are largely neural. Adolescents: peak adaptability post-puberty. Older adults: respond to resistance training similarly to younger but slower; counteracts sarcopenia; emphasize multi-joint, moderate load progressing to heavier. Female athletes: similar relative strength gains; absolute differences mostly from hormonal (testosterone) profile and lean mass distribution. Female athlete triad / RED-S: energy deficiency → menstrual dysfunction → low BMD.
key_facts: Pediatric resistance training is SAFE with supervision
Sarcopenia begins ~30s, accelerates >50
Female-to-male strength ratio: ~60-80% (lower body closer; upper body further)
RED-S: Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport — broader than female athlete triad
Menopause: bone loss accelerates; resistance training is protective
16.training_link: Not directly your case, but understanding RED-S concepts maps to your training load × sleep/energy availability — you've felt the limiter when sleep median is 5.5h.

video.title: Huberman Lab: Galpin — How to Build Strength, Muscle Size & Endurance

audio.title: Stronger By Science Podcast — Episode 9: Hormonal Contraceptives, Training Adaptations

17.title: Sport psychology — arousal, anxiety, motivation
17.key_concept: Arousal: physiological/psychological activation. Inverted-U hypothesis: performance is best at moderate arousal; too low = under-prepared, too high = anxious/tight. Individual Zones of Optimal Functioning (IZOF) refines this — each athlete has unique optimal zone. Anxiety = cognitive (worry) + somatic (physical). Motivation: intrinsic (process/mastery) vs extrinsic (reward); intrinsic is more durable. Goal-setting: SMART, process > outcome. Self-talk and imagery are validated tools.
key_facts: Inverted-U (Yerkes-Dodson): performance × arousal
IZOF (Hanin): individual optimal arousal zones
Process goals > outcome goals for skill acquisition
Imagery + self-talk improve performance (meta-analyses)
Choking: skilled performers under high pressure; relates to attentional control
17.training_link: Race-week tapering isn't just physiological — your IZOF for Hyrox may differ from training; lower-arousal lifters often need to elevate, higher-arousal athletes need to settle.

video.title: Yerkes-Dodson Law — Inverted U Theory | Performance vs. Stress

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: How to Assess & Improve All Aspects of Fitness

18.title: Basic nutrition — macronutrients
18.key_concept: Carbohydrate: 4 kcal/g; spares protein, primary fuel for higher-intensity work; stored as glycogen (~400 g muscle + 100 g liver). Recommendation for athletes: 5-10 g/kg/day depending on training. Protein: 4 kcal/g; structural and enzymatic. Athletes: 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day; up to 2.2 in hypertrophy phases. Fat: 9 kcal/g; essential for hormones, vitamin absorption. Minimum 20-30% kcal from fat. Hydration: ~30-40 mL/kg/day baseline + sweat replacement.
key_facts: CHO: 4 kcal/g; muscle glycogen ~400 g
PRO: 4 kcal/g; athlete intake 1.4-2.0 g/kg
FAT: 9 kcal/g; ≥20% of kcal
Hydration: 30-40 mL/kg + sweat
EAA leucine threshold ~2.5-3 g per meal for MPS
18.training_link: Your training volume (Z2 + intervals + strength + Hyrox) puts CHO needs at high end (~6-8 g/kg). Underfueling chronically blunts everything from glycogen recovery to GH pulse to immune function.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Resistance Training, Body Composition & Hypertrophy

audio.title: Sigma Nutrition #123 — Donald Layman PhD: Leucine, mTOR & Anabolic Response

19.title: Performance nutrition — timing & supplements
19.key_concept: Pre-exercise: CHO 3-4h prior (1-4 g/kg), lower glycemic; small CHO+PRO 30-60 min if tolerated. During: <60 min no need beyond water; 60-90 min: 30-60 g CHO/hr; >2.5 hr: 60-90 g CHO/hr (mixed glucose+fructose). Post: 0.8-1.2 g/kg CHO + 0.25-0.4 g/kg PRO within ~2 hr (the 'anabolic window' is wider than once thought but earlier is better for glycogen). Effective supplements (high evidence): creatine, caffeine, beta-alanine, sodium bicarbonate, beetroot/nitrate, whey/EAA.
key_facts: Creatine: 3-5 g/day, no loading needed
Caffeine: 3-6 mg/kg, 30-60 min pre-exercise
Beta-alanine: 4-6 g/day; buffers H⁺ for 1-4 min efforts
Sodium bicarbonate: 0.3 g/kg; same intensity range as BA
Nitrate (beetroot): 5-9 mmol, 2-3 hr pre
19.training_link: Caffeine 30-60 min before your 4×8 sessions has high-evidence ergogenic effect for sustained high-intensity work in the 2-10 min range.

video.title: Brad Schoenfeld — Resistance Training & Time Efficiency

audio.title: Sigma Nutrition #123 — Leucine Kinetics & mTOR (Layman PhD)

20.title: Performance-enhancing substances & methods
20.key_concept: WADA-banned categories: anabolic agents (T, designer steroids), peptide hormones (GH, EPO), beta-2 agonists, hormone/metabolic modulators, diuretics (masking), stimulants, narcotics, cannabinoids, glucocorticoids (in-competition), blood doping. CSCS exam tests recognition of: mechanisms, side effects, ethical/legal context. Recognized side effects of AAS: HPTA suppression, hepatotoxicity (oral 17α-alkylated), cardiovascular risk, psychological effects, virilization in females.
key_facts: WADA = World Anti-Doping Agency
Anabolic steroids increase MPS via androgen receptor binding
Oral 17α-alkylated steroids = hepatotoxicity
EPO/blood doping ↑ RBC mass → ↑ VO₂max
GH abuse: insulin resistance, acromegaly features
20.training_link: Not applicable. Worth knowing the recognition criteria — coaches are mandated reporters and need to recognize patterns.

video.title: USADA Coach's Advantage — Anti-Doping Education

audio.title: Sigma Nutrition Radio — Performance-Enhancing Substances

21.title: Test selection principles — validity, reliability, specificity
21.key_concept: Validity = test measures what it claims (construct, content, criterion). Reliability = consistency on repeat administration (test-retest, inter-rater). Objectivity = inter-rater reliability specifically. Specificity = tests should resemble sport demands (metabolic, biomechanical). Sequence on a test day: 1) non-fatiguing (vertical jump, agility), 2) agility, 3) max strength (1RM), 4) sprints, 5) muscular endurance, 6) anaerobic capacity, 7) aerobic. Always rest fully between modalities.
key_facts: Validity ≠ reliability (a test can be reliable but invalid)
Order: least to most fatiguing; speed/power → strength → endurance
Standardization is critical for reliability
Test must match the demands of the sport
Pre-test: warm-up, familiarization, instructions identical
21.training_link: Your block testing decisions (decoupling, LT thresholds) follow these same principles — reliability via standardized indoor venue, validity by choosing tests that map to Hyrox demands.

video.title: Andy Galpin — How to Assess & Improve All Aspects of Your Fitness

audio.title: Iron Culture — Brad Schoenfeld: Hypertrophy Research Methods

22.title: Test administration — strength & power
22.key_concept: 1RM testing: thorough warm-up, 2-4 attempts at progressively heavier loads with 2-4 min rest; spotters required. Vertical jump: countermovement vs static; Sargent test or Vertec/jump mat. Standing long jump: distance from takeoff to closest heel landing. Margaria-Kalamen stair sprint: anaerobic power. Wingate: 30s max cycle effort — peak/mean power, fatigue index.
key_facts: 1RM: progressive sets to true 1RM in 4-5 attempts
Vertec valid; jump mats more practical
Margaria-Kalamen: 9 steps, time gates
Wingate: 30s, peak in ~5s, mean = anaerobic capacity
Spotter is non-negotiable for max-load squat/bench
22.training_link: Indoor venue + consistent warm-up = your standardization. The reason your LT2=158 is trustworthy.

video.title: Squat University — How to Squat Correctly (1RM testing context)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Assessment of Fitness

23.title: Test administration — endurance & body comp
23.key_concept: Aerobic: 1.5-mile run, 12-min Cooper, beep test (multistage shuttle), VO₂max via metabolic cart. Anaerobic capacity: 300-yd shuttle. Body comp: skinfolds (Jackson-Pollock 3 or 7-site), BIA, BodPod, DEXA. Skinfold accuracy depends on tester skill; standardized sites and right side.
key_facts: Cooper test: distance in 12 min → VO₂max estimate
Beep test: 20m shuttles, increasing pace
Skinfolds: right side, standardized landmarks
DEXA = gold standard for body comp in field/research
Hydration affects BIA significantly
23.training_link: Field tests good enough for monitoring. Your LT testing is more sensitive than 12-min Cooper for tracking change in trained athletes.

video.title: Seiler — Polarized Training 101 (testing context)

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #85 — San-Millán: Energy Systems & Testing

24.title: Warm-up & flexibility
24.key_concept: General warm-up: 5-10 min low-intensity activity to raise core temp. Specific warm-up: movement patterns of upcoming session. RAMP protocol: Raise, Activate/Mobilize, Potentiate. Static stretching: holds 30s+; can transiently reduce force/power if done immediately pre-performance. Dynamic stretching: movement-based; preferred for performance-day warm-up. PNF: contract-relax variations; high gain but partner-dependent.
key_facts: RAMP: Raise, Activate, Mobilize, Potentiate
Static pre-performance: transient power drop ~5%
Dynamic warm-up preferred for power activities
PNF most effective for ROM gains
Cold muscle = higher injury risk
24.training_link: Your 4×8 sessions and Hyrox prep deserve specific warm-up — rep-1 'too easy' rule means warm-up determines rep-1 readiness.

video.title: Squat University — How To Squat Correctly (FIX MISTAKES) — includes warm-up

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Optimal Protocols (warm-up & potentiation)

25.title: Resistance technique — squat
25.key_concept: Back squat: bar on upper traps (high bar) or rear delts (low bar). Stance ~shoulder width, toes slight out. Cues: brace core (Valsalva for max effort), break at hips and knees together, descend keeping chest up, knees track over toes (not collapsed in). Depth ≥ parallel (femur). Drive through midfoot, hips and shoulders rise together. Spotters at sides for max attempts. Common faults: knee valgus, butt wink at bottom, forward lean.
key_facts: Bar position changes torque distribution between hips/knees
Parallel = femur parallel to floor
Valsalva increases intra-abdominal pressure → spine stability
Knee valgus is the most common fault; cue 'knees out'
Spotters at sides, never directly behind, for max attempts
25.training_link: Your strength PM session structure relies on squat fidelity — these technique points are non-negotiable for your hip-dominant Hyrox prep.

video.title: Squat University — How To Squat Correctly (FIX MISTAKES!)

audio.title: Iron Culture — Schoenfeld: Mechanisms of Hypertrophy

26.title: Resistance technique — deadlift
26.key_concept: Conventional: stance ~hip width, shins near bar, grip outside knees (double-overhand or mixed), hips set below shoulders, neutral spine, lats engaged. Pull: bar drags shins, push floor away with legs, hips and shoulders rise together until bar passes knees then hips extend through. Sumo: wider stance, grip inside knees, more vertical torso, more quad/glute, less low-back demand. Trap bar: more upright torso, friendlier to most lifters.
key_facts: Bar should travel in straight line, close to body
Neutral spine throughout
Lockout: stand tall, hips extended; don't hyperextend lumbar
Sumo reduces low-back shear, increases adductor demand
Trap bar reduces L4-L5 shear
26.training_link: Trap bar deadlift is the highest-ROI lift for your hybrid demands — high power output, low joint cost.

video.title: Squat University — How to Deadlift Properly (Conventional & Sumo)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Strength Protocols

27.title: Resistance technique — bench & overhead press
27.key_concept: Bench: 5 points of contact (head, upper back, glutes on bench; both feet on floor). Grip ~1.5× shoulder width. Bar path: descend to lower chest/sternum, slight curve up to over upper chest at lockout. Scaps retracted/depressed. Spotter at head. Overhead press: bar at clavicle, elbows slightly in front, brace, press up while staying tight, slight forward shift of head as bar passes face. Avoid lumbar hyperextension.
key_facts: Bench: 5 points of contact
Scaps retracted and depressed
Bar to lower chest, NOT throat
OHP: brace HARD; lumbar neutral
Push-press uses leg drive to start
27.training_link: OHP is technique-heavy and underused — but for shoulder durability across a long training career it's worth keeping in.

video.title: Squat University — How To Squat Correctly (technique principles)

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Maximizing Hypertrophy

28.title: Olympic lifts — power clean & derivatives
28.key_concept: Power clean: from floor or hang. Phases: 1st pull (floor to knee, slow), transition (knee to mid-thigh, hips extend), 2nd pull (explosive triple extension — ankle, knee, hip), catch (front rack, elbows high, partial squat). Common faults: early arm bend, jumping forward, soft catch. Hang clean: starts from mid-thigh — teaches 2nd pull. Mid-thigh pull: power without catch demand. Olympic derivatives may give better ROI than full lifts for sport athletes.
key_facts: Triple extension: ankle, knee, hip
Arms stay straight in 1st and 2nd pull
Catch: front rack, elbows high
Hang clean starts from mid-thigh
Derivatives often superior for non-Olympic sports
28.training_link: Likely overkill for Hyrox — high learning cost vs return. Kettlebell swing or jump squat may deliver triple-extension power with lower complexity.

video.title: Galpin — Optimal Protocols (power & technique)

audio.title: Iron Culture 236 — Galpin: Muscle Fiber Types & Training

29.title: Alternative modes — kettlebell, sled, strongman
29.key_concept: KB swing: hip-hinge driven; bell floats with hip extension; not a squat. Goblet squat: front-loaded squat — good teaching tool. Turkish get-up: full-body integration. Sled push/drag: low eccentric load, high metabolic. Strongman (yoke, farmers, sandbag): odd-object, irregular loading; high-stability demand. Each has place in athletic training. Selection: match demand profile to sport.
key_facts: KB swing: hip hinge, NOT a squat
Sled: low eccentric → low DOMS, can do daily
Farmers: grip, posture, glute endurance
Yoke walk: spinal compression demand
Odd objects build trunk stability
29.training_link: Sled push is highly Hyrox-specific. Low eccentric load means recovery cost is lower than the metabolic stress would suggest — useful as substitute for higher-impact intervals.

video.title: Squat University — Deadlift Form Fixes (alternative implements)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Strength Building Protocols

30.title: Resistance program design — needs analysis
30.key_concept: Step 1 of program design. Two-part: (a) Sport analysis — movement patterns, primary energy systems, injury patterns, common positions. (b) Athlete analysis — training history, injuries, testing data, goals, training age. Inputs from steps 1-2 drive everything downstream: exercise selection, intensity, volume, frequency, rest, periodization. Without needs analysis, programming is generic.
key_facts: Sport analysis: movements, energy systems, injuries, common positions
Athlete analysis: history, injuries, testing, goals, training age
Drives exercise selection, intensity, volume, frequency, rest
Periodization is downstream of needs analysis
Re-assess needs analysis as the athlete evolves
30.training_link: Your Hyrox needs analysis: hip-dominant, mixed metabolic, station-strength endurance, heat-tolerance, low joint-impact for runs. Maps directly to your current substitution rules.

video.title: Galpin — Optimal Protocols (needs analysis discussion)

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Maximizing Hypertrophy (programming)

31.title: Resistance program — exercise selection
31.key_concept: Core exercises (multi-joint, large muscle): squat, deadlift, bench, OHP, row. Assistance (single-joint or smaller muscle). Structural exercises load the spine axially; power exercises are explosive (cleans, jumps). Sport-specific exercises mimic competitive movement patterns. Balance pushing/pulling, anterior/posterior, knee/hip dominance. Exercise order: power → core → assistance (most fatiguing → least).
key_facts: Core: multi-joint, large muscle group, structural
Order: power → core → assistance
Balance push/pull, ant/post, knee/hip
Sport-specific should resemble actual movement
Single-joint exercises target weak links
31.training_link: Your sled push + KB swing + hinge variations are core/power; accessory single-joint lifts fill gaps.

video.title: Mike Israetel — How Much Training Volume is Best? (Mesocycle Design)

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Hypertrophy Programming

32.title: Resistance program — intensity & volume
32.key_concept: %1RM and rep ranges: strength 1-6 reps at 85-100%; hypertrophy 6-12 at 65-85%; muscular endurance 12+ at <65%; power 1-5 explosive at varied intensities (30-50% for ballistic, 75-90% for cleans). Volume = sets × reps × load. Weekly hard-set landmark (Schoenfeld): MV (maintenance) ≈ 6-10, MEV ≈ 10, MAV ≈ 12-20, MRV ≈ 20+ per muscle/wk for hypertrophy. Strength MRV lower.
key_facts: Strength: 1-6 reps, 85-100% 1RM, 3-5 sets, long rest
Hypertrophy: 6-12 reps, 65-85%, 3-6 sets
Endurance: 12+ reps, <65%, 2-3 sets
Power: 1-5 reps explosive, varied %
Volume landmarks: MV/MEV/MAV/MRV
32.training_link: Your strength PM sessions should sit in low-rep, moderate-set range (5×5, 4×6 patterns) — high stimulus, low recovery cost stacked on top of your conditioning load.

video.title: Dr. Mike Israetel — How Much Training Volume is Best? (MEV/MAV/MRV)

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Volume & Frequency for Hypertrophy

33.title: Resistance program — rest & frequency
33.key_concept: Rest intervals: power and strength 3-5 min between sets; hypertrophy 1-3 min; endurance ≤1 min. Short rest reduces load capacity; long rest preserves intensity. Frequency: per muscle 2-3×/wk produces more hypertrophy than 1×/wk at equated volume (meta-analyses). For strength, 3-5×/wk possible at lower per-session volume. Recovery between full-body sessions: 48 hr minimum.
key_facts: Strength/power: 3-5 min rest
Hypertrophy: 1-3 min rest
Endurance: ≤1 min
2-3×/wk per muscle > 1×/wk at equated volume
Strength can tolerate higher frequency than max-hypertrophy programs
33.training_link: Your 'recovery by session type' memory matches this — strength PM days are 2×/wk, leaving 48+ hr recovery between similar stimuli.

video.title: Mike Israetel — Volume Landmarks & Mesocycle Design

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Frequency & Recovery

34.title: Plyometric program design
34.key_concept: SSC (stretch-shortening cycle): eccentric pre-load enhances concentric output via stored elastic energy + stretch reflex. Amortization phase = transition; SHORTER amortization = better. Categories: low-intensity (skipping, ankle hops), moderate (box jumps, hurdle hops), high (depth jumps, repeat broad jumps). Volume measured in foot contacts: beginner 80-100/session; advanced 120-140; elite ≤200. Surface: grass/track > concrete. Pre-req: ~1.5× BW squat for high-intensity plyos.
key_facts: SSC: eccentric → amortization → concentric
Amortization: faster = better SSC use
Foot contacts: 80-200/session
Surface affects injury risk
1.5× BW squat as pre-req for depth jumps
34.training_link: Your hip-dominant strength base supports plyo addition — but high-impact plyos (depth jumps) compete for joint capacity with your run volume. Box jumps (low landing impact) are safer add.

video.title: Galpin — Optimal Protocols (plyometric programming)

audio.title: Iron Culture — Hypertrophy & Power Adaptations

35.title: Speed & agility training
35.key_concept: Sprint mechanics: acceleration (0-20m, forward lean, long push), max velocity (upright, cyclic, vertical force production), deceleration. Coaching cues: arm drive, knee drive, dorsiflexion. Agility = ability to react and change direction; CODS = closed-loop change of direction. Reactive agility involves stimulus (visual/auditory). Programming: short distances at full intensity, full recovery (work:rest 1:12-1:20 for max speed).
key_facts: Acceleration: forward lean, long ground contact
Max velocity: upright, vertical force, brief contact
Work:rest for max-speed = 1:12 to 1:20
Reactive agility > closed-loop for transfer to sport
Sprint training is a power-skill — not a conditioning tool
35.training_link: Hyrox running is sub-maximal pacing — true speed work won't transfer much. Skip dedicated speed unless you're chasing a 5K PR.

video.title: Galpin — Assess & Improve All Aspects of Fitness (speed work)

audio.title: Iron Culture 236 — Galpin: Fiber Types & Training Specificity

36.title: Aerobic endurance program design
36.key_concept: FITT-VP: Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type, Volume, Progression. Modes: LSD (long slow distance), tempo (LT2 work), interval (work:rest at high intensity), Fartlek (fartlek = play with speed). Polarized model (Seiler): ~80% sessions in Z1/2 (below LT1), ~20% in Z3+ (above LT2), minimum time in the middle 'gray zone.' Periodization: progressive volume → progressive intensity → race specificity → taper.
key_facts: FITT-VP framework
LSD: 30+ min at <70% HRmax
Tempo: at LT2, 20-40 min
Polarized: 80/20 by SESSION COUNT (Seiler)
Pyramidal (more tempo, less Z3+) also used
36.training_link: Your block follows Seiler closely (memory: session-count basis for 80/20). 4×8 at HR 170-175 is your supra-MLSS work; Z2 cycling is your Z1/2 bulk.

video.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training in Practice

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — San-Millán: Zone 2 Programming

37.title: Periodization I — models
37.key_concept: Linear (Matveyev): high volume/low intensity → low volume/high intensity over a macrocycle. Reverse linear: inverse, for endurance. Undulating (DUP): intensity/volume vary within a week — strong evidence for strength gains. Block (Issurin): sequential focused blocks of accumulation → transmutation → realization. Conjugate (Westside): rotating max-effort + dynamic-effort + repeated-effort methods in the same week.
key_facts: Linear: traditional, single-peak
Undulating: daily/weekly variation
Block: sequential focused blocks
Conjugate: simultaneous methods, rotating exercises
DUP often outperforms linear for strength in trained lifters
37.training_link: Your block structure (memory: v9.8 phased plan, Phase 0 sleep gate, 4-week blocks) is block periodization with explicit readiness gates — most CSCS texts don't include readiness gates; you've gone beyond textbook.

video.title: Mike Israetel — Mesocycle Design & Volume Landmarks

audio.title: Iron Culture 51 — Schoenfeld: Periodization for Hypertrophy

38.title: Periodization II — macro, meso, micro
38.key_concept: Macrocycle: annual or season-long plan. Mesocycle: 2-6 wk blocks with a specific focus (hypertrophy, strength, power, peaking). Microcycle: typically 1 week. Phases (linear): hypertrophy/endurance → basic strength → strength/power → peaking → active rest. Tapering: 7-21 days, ~40-60% volume reduction, intensity maintained, frequency maintained. Supercompensation: rest → recovery → adaptation → return to baseline.
key_facts: Macro: months to year
Meso: 2-6 weeks
Micro: ~1 week
Taper: cut volume ~50%, keep intensity
Supercompensation timing varies by stimulus
38.training_link: Your block weeks → 4-week meso → race buildup pattern matches this textbook framework. Phase 0 sleep gate is custom on top.

video.title: Stephen Seiler — Polarized Training 201: Past, Present & Future

audio.title: Peter Attia Drive #201 — San-Millán: Zone 2 (block periodization)

39.title: Rehabilitation & reconditioning principles
39.key_concept: Tissue healing phases: inflammation (0-6 days), proliferation (3 days-3 wks), remodeling (3 wks-1 yr+). PT-led acute care; CSCS role in return-to-sport (RTS) bridging. RTS criteria: pain-free ROM, ≥90% LSI (limb symmetry index), psychological readiness, sport-specific testing. Progressive loading: isometric → isotonic → isokinetic → SSC/plyometric → sport-specific. Avoid rushing the SSC stage.
key_facts: Healing phases: inflammation, proliferation, remodeling
LSI ≥90% for RTS
Progressive loading sequence non-negotiable
Psychological readiness is real and measurable
CSCS works WITH PT, not replacing
39.training_link: Reads as preventive for you — load progression rules apply to ANY new stimulus, not just injury return.

video.title: Squat University — How to Deadlift Properly (return-to-lifting form)

audio.title: Huberman Lab — Galpin: Recovery Protocols

40.title: Facility design & layout
40.key_concept: 100 sq ft per athlete minimum. Ceiling: 12+ ft (Olympic lifts) or 10 ft minimum. Lighting: 50-100 foot-candles. Flooring: rubber for free-weight areas, low-pile carpet OK for cardio. Mirrors: 6 in above floor, 20 in clear of equipment. Equipment spacing: 36 in walkways. Power racks anchored. Layout flow: stretch → free weight → resistance → cardio (or per traffic patterns).
key_facts: 100 sq ft per athlete
Ceiling: 12 ft (Oly), 10 ft minimum
Lighting: 50-100 foot-candles
Walkways: 36 in minimum
Equipment grouped by movement pattern
40.training_link: Relevant if you ever open a facility — exam-relevant numbers to memorize verbatim.

video.title: Galpin — Assess & Improve Fitness (facility considerations)

audio.title: Iron Culture — Hypertrophy & Training Environment

41.title: Facility policies, procedures & legal
41.key_concept: Negligence requires: duty, breach of duty, causation, damages. Standard of care = what a reasonable professional in same role would do. Documentation is everything (waivers, PARQ, incident reports, equipment maintenance logs). Emergency action plan (EAP): venue-specific, posted, practiced. Pre-participation screening (PARQ, medical clearance for risk factors). Confidentiality (HIPAA/FERPA where applicable).
key_facts: Negligence: duty, breach, causation, damages
Standard of care = reasonable professional in same role
EAP required and practiced
PARQ before participation
Equipment inspection logged
41.training_link: Not direct for you, but recurring exam content area.

video.title: USADA Coach's Advantage — NSCA Continuing Education

audio.title: Sigma Nutrition Radio — Evidence-based practice

42.title: Phase 1 integration review
42.key_concept: Phase 1 spans Exercise Sciences foundations. Today: stitch together. Muscle structure → motor units → EC coupling → cardio/respiratory → bioenergetics (3 systems) → biomechanics → endocrine → adaptations. Notice the through-line: ALL training response comes back to stress → recovery → adaptation, mediated by specific tissue and system responses.
key_facts: Force production: muscle architecture × neural drive × biomechanics
Energy supply: phosphagen (10s) → glycolysis (2min) → oxidative (>2min)
Hormones modulate adaptation; sleep modulates hormones
